reasoning: This sequence replicates the laid-back 'shaka' or 'hang loose' gesture with the right arm, incorporating small wrist twists to mimic the fluidity of the gesture. The left arm remains mostly relaxed throughout, with subtle micro-movements to avoid stiffness. I’ll ensure the right arm’s movements feel natural by checking examples and making sure the transitions are smooth. In the first frame, both arms are positioned in a neutral, relaxed state. The right arm is slightly raised with the wrist neutral, ready for the movement, while the left arm hangs loosely at the side with minimal movement to keep the posture natural. In the second frame, the right arm moves upward, twisting the wrist outward at -90°, simulating the start of the iconic 'shaka' gesture. Joint1 rolls the arm back slightly while Joint2 raises it. The left arm remains still to balance the pose. By the third frame, the right wrist twists inward to 90°, maintaining the movement's fluidity. Joint1 and Joint2 adjust to bring the right arm slightly forward, as if waving the hand. The left arm subtly adjusts to match the slight shift in balance. In the fourth frame, the right arm moves slightly upward again, with the wrist twisting back outward at -90°, continuing the back-and-forth motion. Small adjustments in Joint1 help maintain the rhythm of the gesture. Finally, in the fifth frame, the right arm returns to the inward twist at 90°, maintaining the 'shaka' flow. Throughout the sequence, the micro-movements of the left arm keep the robot's posture from appearing too rigid while letting the right arm do the expressive work.
